dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI edged out the field because Codex got a concrete endorsement in the Edge.js thread, where the author said it compressed a year-plus build into a few weeks, while the main drag on the day was the broader HN backlash against Codex-mediated OSS interactions in the Django discussion [E6][E4].
winnerExplanation: OpenAI won by posting the least damaged direct signal among tracked providers: one clear, product-specific claim that Codex materially accelerated Edge.js development [E6], against one negative thread about contributors using Codex to answer review feedback in open source [E4]. Anthropic drew more total discussion, but the praise for Claude in 3D and coding workflows was offset by stronger criticism around token burn, plan limits, and Claude-generated patches in shared projects [E1][E2][E3][E5].

openai.judgementSnippet: Codex got one direct boost from the Edge.js author, who said it helped turn a year-plus project into a few weeks, but HN also pushed back on contributors using Codex to handle PR feedback, arguing that it makes reviewers feel like they are talking to a facade instead of a collaborator [E6][E4].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Claude drew real praise for helping with 3D modeling and for being useful enough that some users preferred steering Claude Code directly, but the bigger HN headwinds were complaints about token burn, max-plan limits, and discomfort with Claude-generated code being pushed into shared open-source projects [E1][E2][E3][E5].

evidence.summary: In the Get Shit Done thread, commenters said Claude Code was good enough that they preferred steering it directly, but spec-driven wrappers around it could burn 10x more tokens and hit Max plan limits without clear gains.

The Claude Tips for 3D Work discussion included multiple firsthand reports that Claude produced elegant FreeCAD/Python code, handled complex geometry well, and even adjusted camera views to improve results.

In the Node.js virtual file system thread, top comments attacked a large PR mostly generated by Claude Code, calling that against project norms and saying AI-authored changes leave shared infrastructure feeling tainted.

The Django anti-token thread criticized contributors who have Claude or Codex answer review comments for them, with commenters saying this shifts burden onto maintainers and makes review feel like talking to a facade.
The Django anti-token thread also directly named Claude in complaints about AI-mediated pull requests, framing that behavior as demoralizing for reviewers and harmful to open-source collaboration.
In the Edge.js launch thread, the Wasmer author said the team used AI and Codex heavily and that doing so compressed a project that otherwise would have taken a year or more.
